---
title: "pyMETRIC-UAV: Getting Started"
subtitle: "Initial setup and a working example"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
---

This guide walks a first-time user from a clean computer to a finished
evapotranspiration (ET) map, using the bundled **Fruita_Example** dataset.
It assumes no prior Python experience: you create the software environment
once, then open one notebook and run it top to bottom.

For the science behind the model, the configuration reference, and the
validation against the reference `WSWUP/pyMETRIC` code, see the companion `pyMETRIC_UAV_overview.pdf`
document.

# What you need before starting

- The **project folder** (the directory that contains `pyMETRIC/`,
  `pyMETRIC_Batch_Run.ipynb`, `environment.yml`, and `Batch_Data/`). Keep this
  folder intact --- the notebook loads its model code directly from the
  bundled `pyMETRIC/` subfolder.
- **Miniconda or Anaconda** installed. If you do not have it, install Miniconda
  from <https://docs.conda.io/en/latest/miniconda.html>. This is the only
  software you install by hand.

You will use a terminal **once** to create the environment. After that,
everything happens inside the notebook.

# One-time environment setup

The project ships an `environment.yml` that pins every package to the versions
the model was developed and validated against (Python 3.10, GDAL 3.11, pyTSEB
2.1, and the scientific stack). Creating the environment from this file is the
only setup step.

Open a terminal (on Windows, use the **Anaconda Prompt**), change the directory to the
project folder where `pymetric-uav` is located, and run:

```bash
conda env create -f environment.yml
conda activate pymetric-uav
```

The first command downloads and installs everything; it can take several
minutes. The second activates the environment so the next commands use it.

> **Note.** GDAL (the geospatial library) is installed from `conda-forge` by
> the environment file. Do **not** try to install GDAL with `pip` --- mixing
> the two is the most common cause of a broken environment.

Then launch Jupyter from the same terminal:

```bash
jupyter lab
```

This opens Jupyter in your web browser. In the file panel on the left,
double-click **`pyMETRIC_Batch_Run.ipynb`** to open the notebook.

> **You only do this once.** After the environment exists, future sessions are
> just: `conda activate pymetric-uav`, then `jupyter lab`. You do not recreate
> the environment or install anything again.

# Confirming the example dataset is in place

The notebook processes whatever dataset folders it finds in `Batch_Data/`. The
project ships with one example ready to go:

```
Batch_Data/
  Fruita_Example/
    config.txt                 site, meteorology, and band assignments
    example_image_clipped.tif  the multiband UAV image (reflectance + thermal)
    Input/                     endmember / study-plot polygons (and derived
                               rasters, created on the first run)
    Output/                    results land here
```

`config.txt` is the file you edit for your own flights. For Fruita
it is already filled in. The values that matter most are the acquisition time
and the weather at the time of the UAV flight(s):

| Setting | Value | Meaning |
|:--------|:------|:--------|
| `DOY` / `time` | 221 / 10.5 | Aug 9, 10:30 AM local standard time |
| `T_A1` | 27.38 °C | air temperature at the flight |
| `u` | 1.63 m/s | wind speed |
| `S_dn` | 528.25 W/m² | incoming shortwave radiation |
| `crop_type` | `alfalfa` | selects the alfalfa canopy preset |
| `reference_type` | `tall` | alfalfa (ETr) reference surface |
| `endmember_mode` | `zone` | restrict hot/cold search to the drawn polygons |
| `endmember_search` | `2` | Min/Max (percentile-based) selector |

# Running the notebook

The notebook contains six cells. Run them in order (**Shift+Enter** runs the current
cell and moves to the next, or use *Run → Run All Cells*). Each cell print a log and a record 
of any generated output, so you can confirm progress as you go.

## Setup

The first cell imports the libraries and loads the model code from the bundled
`pyMETRIC/` folder. A successful run prints something like:

```
pyMETRIC-batch v4.0.0
Using bundled pyMETRIC: .../pyMETRIC-uav/pyMETRIC
Base directory:  .../pyMETRIC-uav
Batch directory: .../pyMETRIC-uav/Batch_Data (exists: True)
```

The line **`Using bundled pyMETRIC`**  confirms the
notebook is running this folder's model code, not some other copy of pyMETRIC that might be
installed on the machine. If instead the cell stops with an error saying it is
"NOT running the bundled code," the project folder layout has been disturbed ---
make sure `pyMETRIC/` sits next to the notebook, then restart the kernel
(*Kernel → Restart Kernel*) and run this cell first.

## Pre-flight

The second cell scans `Batch_Data/`, checks each dataset's configuration, and
reports which are ready. For the Fruita_Example, you should see it marked **READY**. You should also see a
config sanity check that echoes the acquisition date in plain English (and warns
if any met value looks unusual):

```
=== Preflight Check ===
  [Fruita_Example] READY — multiband: example_image_clipped.tif (config: config.txt)

1/1 datasets ready for processing.

=== Config sanity check ===
  Acquisition: DOY 221, 10.50 hr -> Aug 9 at 10:30 (local standard time)
```

Nothing is processed yet --- this is the safety check.

## Run

The third cell does the actual work. For each ready dataset, this cell:

1. Derives NDVI, leaf area index (LAI), fractional cover, and surface
   temperature (TRAD) from the multiband image and exports each surface to the `Input/` directory;
2. Runs the METRIC energy-balance model;
3. Converts latent heat to instantaneous ET (mm/hr);
4. Computes the ASCE reference ET for the scene; and
5. Computes per-plot zonal statistics from the study-plot polygons.

A plot is provided that indicates the two anchor (endmember) pixels and
the resulting near-surface temperature-gradient line. For the Fruita_Example,
the run reports:

```
Cold pixel at (554, 376): 293.80 K, 0.929 VI
Hot pixel  at (28, 299):  321.85 K, 0.118 VI
Endmember quality OK: LST range = 28.0 K, cold VI = 0.93, hot VI = 0.12
Calibrated dT: a=-27.740023, b=0.095559
  ET mean = 0.2279 mm/hr
  ETr = 0.5267 mm/hr
```

This output indicates selection of a well-watered alfalfa **cold** pixel near 294 K
and a hot bare-soil pixel near 322 K, The image-mean
instantaneous ET is **0.228 mm/hr**, and the ASCE alfalfa reference ET is
**0.527 mm/hr**. The scene averages about 43% of reference, consistent
with a mix of irrigated alfalfa and bare ground.

## Summary

The fourth cell prints a table with one row per dataset in the `Batch/` directory. Each row
contains values for processing status, mean ET, reference ET (ETr), the ET / ETr ratio, and the number of study plots. For
the Fruita_Example, it reads:

```
=== Batch Summary ===
       Dataset  Status  Mean ET (mm/hr)  Ref ETr (mm/hr)  ET / ETr  Plots
Fruita_Example SUCCESS           0.2279           0.5267     0.433     24
```

This table provides a quick "did it work and are the numbers sensible" check.

## Diagnostics (optional)

The fifth cell prints per-raster statistics (min/max/mean/std and NaN counts)
for the derived inputs. These outputs are useful if a result looks off and you want to see
whether NDVI, LAI, and TRAD came out in a plausible range. For the Fruita_Example, TRAD
runs between 293--330 K, NDVI is between 0.03--0.94, and LAI is between 0--7.7. Each includes roughly 6.5% of
pixels masked as no-data.

## Sensitivity (optional)

The sixth cell re-runs the scene under several endmember-selection strategies
(Min/Max, CIMEC, ESA; with and without the zone polygons) and writes a small
table comparing the image-mean ET each one produces. It quantifies how much the
answer depends on how the hot/cold pixels are selected. For the Fruita_Example, the five
strategies span roughly 0.18--0.24 mm/hr. This cell is optional and takes longer to run
because it processes each scene several times.

# What you get

After a successful run, `Batch_Data/Fruita_Example/Output/` contains:

| File | Contents |
|:-----|:---------|
| `Fruita_Example_METRIC.tif` | 4-band raster: net radiation $R_n$, sensible heat $H$, latent heat $LE$, soil heat flux $G$ (all W/m²) |
| `Fruita_Example_ET_mm_hour.tif` | instantaneous ET (mm/hr) --- the headline map |
| `Fruita_Example_METRIC_ancillary.tif` | supporting bands (aerodynamic resistance, reference ET, ET fraction, quality flag, ...) |
| `Fruita_Example_Penman_ET.csv` | the scene's ASCE reference ET |
| `Fruita_Example_Zonal_Stats.csv` | per-study-plot ET and ET-fraction statistics |
| `Fruita_Example_log.txt` | a full text log of the run |

The ET map and the per-plot CSV are usually what you came for. Open the
`*_ET_mm_hour.tif` in any GIS (QGIS, ArcGIS) to view the ET map; open the
`*_Zonal_Stats.csv` in a spreadsheet.

# Running your own flight

To process your own UAV scene instead of the Fruita_Example:

1. Make a new folder inside `Batch_Data/` (e.g. `Batch_Data/MyField/`).
2. Put your multiband image in it and copy `config_template.txt` to
   `MyField/config.txt`. The configuration file is documented in full in the companion overview
   (*Configuration* section); start from the annotated `config_template.txt` and
   change one section at a time.
3. Edit `config.txt`. At minimum, edit the band assignments, the acquisition
   `DOY`/`time`, the four met values (`T_A1`, `u`, `ea`, `S_dn`), the site
   `lat`/`lon`/`alt`, and the `crop_type`. The template is annotated section by
   section.
4. (Optional) Provide hot/cold zone polygons and study-plot polygons in an
   `Input/` subfolder, and set `endmember_mode=zone`.
5. Run the notebook exactly as above. It will pick up every ready dataset in
   `Batch_Data/`, including the Fruita_Example and any additional sets you added. 
6. Once you are satisfied with the model results from one or more datasets, move those 
   directories to a different location (e.g. `Batch_Data/MyField/` → `Processed_Data/MyField/`) so that the model does
   reprocess them each time you add a new dataset. 


# Troubleshooting

| Symptom | Likely cause / fix |
|:--------|:-------------------|
| Setup cell errors: "NOT running the bundled code" | `pyMETRIC/` is not next to the notebook. Restore the folder layout; restart the kernel; run the Setup cell first. |
| `conda env create` fails | Make sure you ran it from the project folder (where `environment.yml` lives) and that conda is installed. Re-run; transient download failures are common. |
| GDAL / `osgeo` import error | The environment did not build correctly. Recreate it from `environment.yml`; do not `pip install gdal`. |
| Pre-flight marks a dataset NOT READY | A required file or `config.txt` key is missing; the preflight message names which one. |
| ET map is mostly zero or NaN | Check the Diagnostics cell --- usually a bad band assignment (wrong red/NIR/thermal band number) or out-of-range surface temperatures. |
